notes: Olsen stated 120000 people working together was 'inconceivable' but achieved; validates DEC at absolute employment peak mid-1988
DEC achieved 24% growth in FY87; strong rebound from 14% growth year before; described as out-performing virtually all competition
FY87 operating profit margin was 17%, a giant leap from 11% in FY86 and well in excess of budget; peak margin performance

metric_value: 2.1 billion USD
notes: $2.1B cash after spending ~$800M to repurchase stock in FY87; triple-A credit rating; Fortune 500 rank moved from 44th to 38th

metric_value: approximately $170/share
notes: Stock had traded at ~$170/share a year prior (mid-1987); declined substantially after Oct 19 1987 crash; Osterhoff characterized recent performance as 'disappointing'
First three quarters FY88 revenue up 21%; below plan but near historical average since 1981; cost growth exceeded revenue growth in Q2 and Q3

metric_value: 1.5 billion USD/year
notes: CapEx jumped sharply to ~$1.5B/year from ~$750M; Osterhoff flagged as 'warning signal' that cost pressures would persist; includes office space purchases previously leased
Q1 FY88 pretax margins ~13.5%, same as Q1 FY87; Q2 dropped 3 percentage points below prior year; Q3 dropped 5 points — margin compression worsening

metric_value: One Company One Strategy One Message — Leading the Way to Enterprise-Wide Computing
notes: Official strategy theme for May 1988 State of the Company; Olsen said strategy evolved from 'many product lines competing' to unified resources; claimed miraculous execution

metric_value: DEC deliberately chose not to compete in personal computers for the home; positioned this as a strategic strength not weakness
notes: CRITICALLY WRONG PREDICTION: Olsen said 'Rather than just be one of hundreds of companies making personal computers... I'd rather be different and take all the arrows.' The PC platform ultimately destroyed DEC's market. Fatal strategic error documented at its moment of articulation.

metric_value: Market surveys are a serious danger; customers only want what they have seen; that's not where new ideas come from
notes: Olsen cited Sony Walkman as example of ignoring market surveys. Irony: this philosophy contributed to DEC ignoring clear customer demand signals for PCs and Unix workstations in the late 1980s.

metric_value: VMS is more modern than Unix — 12 years old vs Unix 19 years old; VMS designed for the whole job to last almost forever
notes: Technically arguable but strategically irrelevant: Unix won the enterprise computing market through open standards and RISC workstations. VMS survived as niche. Olsen's framing failed to anticipate customer preference for vendor independence over architectural elegance.

metric_value: Sell VMS and Unix (ULTRIX) with equal enthusiasm; they are not equal but serve different purposes
notes: Olsen acknowledged this modified the 'one message' concept; dual OS strategy created internal conflict and resource dilution; ULTRIX ultimately discontinued

metric_value: Strengthen position relative to IBM and assume leadership role in enterprise-wide information systems
notes: Strecker's explicit framing: DEC's entire strategy was IBM-displacement; six focus areas: applications environment, TP/databases, desktop systems, enterprise networking, industry-standard OS, non-DEC architecture integration

metric_value: OSI networking standards will replace IBM SNA as enterprise networking standard; SNA described as closed, complex, hierarchical — not meeting real needs
notes: PARTIALLY CORRECT: OSI protocols became influential standards but TCP/IP (not OSI) ultimately won the internet and enterprise networking wars. SNA was displaced but by TCP/IP not OSI. DEC's bet on OSI was strategically reasonable but wrong protocol.

metric_value: MS-DOS, OS/2, IBM SNA, Cray supercomputing, and Apple Macintosh named as non-DEC architecture targets for NAS
notes: Shows DEC's sophisticated multi-platform integration strategy; notably includes Apple (announced joint development); VMS Services for MS-DOS described as leading integration product

metric_value: 60 billion USD (40% of worldwide computer revenue)
notes: Desktop devices + servers + networking + services estimated at $60B by 1991 = 40%+ of worldwide computer revenue; shows DEC's awareness of desktop market scale even while underinvesting in it

metric_value: Analysts predicted death of terminals replaced by PCs — that didn't happen; terminals remain preferred for many applications; shipping 500000+ terminals this year
notes: EVENTUALLY WRONG: Terminal market did survive longer than predicted (through mid-1990s) but PCs ultimately did replace terminals for most office use. DEC's terminal revenue was genuinely strong in 1988 — VT320/VT330/VT340 all exceeded forecasts.

metric_value: DEC became fastest-growing workstation vendor in industry FY1988; surpassed Sun Microsystems growth rate; leaped over Apollo to number two position
notes: Accurate at time of speech; however 90% of DEC workstations ran VMS vs industry shift to Unix; LaCava acknowledged this as 'serious challenge' — DEC never captured Unix workstation market

metric_value: Seven times more commercial computing users than technical users; DEC must win commercial desktop business while keeping technical users
notes: FAILED: DEC never successfully penetrated commercial desktop market; VAXstation remained expensive technical workstation; Windows-based PCs captured the commercial desktop

metric_value: Converting an IBM PC-AT to run OS/2 may cost as much as $8000 (per Computerworld March 7 issue)
notes: Used to argue DEC's architectural consistency was competitive advantage vs costly PC OS transitions. OS/2 ultimately failed for different reasons; Windows 3.x resolved compatibility issues more cheaply.

metric_value: VMS Services for MS-DOS is the leading integration product in the market; won numerous awards
notes: DEC genuinely had strong PC integration products; however the strategic bet on integrating PCs into DEC enterprise (rather than vice versa) was ultimately wrong direction

metric_value: DEC announced joint development with Apple; bringing Apple desktop systems into DEC enterprise-wide computing environment; 'just one part of our approach'
notes: Partnership announced; notable that DEC framed Apple as integration target rather than competitor; reflects DEC's self-perception as enterprise backbone

metric_value: Common user interface + common programming interface + X Windows industry standard + quick delivery — four-part workstation software strategy
notes: Architecturally sound strategy; X Windows was a genuine technical win; however DECwindows lost to Microsoft Windows 3.x commercially. Heinen later joined Microsoft.

metric_value: As DECwindows becomes a de facto standard... the environment will become that much more productive
notes: FAILED: DECwindows did not become a de facto standard; Microsoft Windows 3.0 (1990) captured the GUI market. X Windows became standard in Unix workstations but not commercial desktop.

metric_value: X Windows managed by MIT-sponsored X Windows Consortium; DEC is member along with 23 other prominent computer vendors
notes: Accurate; DEC was a founding member of X Consortium; X Window System v11 (X11) was widely adopted in Unix workstation world. Heinen claimed 'we picked the winner' — correct for Unix world, wrong for commercial desktop.

metric_value: Over 50 internal software projects implementing DECwindows applications; hundreds of active users; training hundreds of external developers
notes: Demonstrates genuine internal momentum; however DECwindows V1.0 shipping in 1988 faced slow external ISV adoption and performance issues vs competition

metric_value: Transaction processing market approximately $26 billion; growing at 20-30% per year; expected to reach $60 billion by 1991
notes: Market sizing articulated by DEC internally; directionally accurate — TP/OLTP market was indeed massive and growing. The $60B 1991 figure proved somewhat optimistic.

metric_value: 90-95% of TP actions must complete in 1-2 seconds; systems are 'bet your business' applications requiring near-zero downtime
notes: Accurate technical characterization of OLTP requirements; DEC's articulation was sophisticated and correct; this framing drove the VAXcluster high-availability architecture

metric_value: Industry trend toward using databases (RDBMS) for TP storage replacing flat files; performance gap closing though flat files remain faster
notes: CORRECT PREDICTION: RDBMS did indeed replace flat-file TP systems over the following decade. Oracle, Sybase, and DEC Rdb all benefited. DEC sold Rdb to Oracle in 1994.

metric_value: DATAMATION survey: customers report 1.3% of their TP systems currently from DEC; expect 10.4% from DEC by end of next year
notes: Remarkable demand signal cited as evidence of TP momentum; the 8x growth expectation from customers reflected genuine market interest in DEC TP offerings in 1988

metric_value: Digital is a leader in transaction processing with enterprise-wide networking and VAXcluster family; DECtp announced as major product initiative
notes: This is the formal DECtp announcement; Peter Kastner led marketing work behind this. Glorioso positioned DEC as having 'market demand, products, performance leadership, price leadership'

metric_value: Digital Distributed Transaction Architecture: front-end (stimulus capture/forms), transaction server (monitor), resource manager (database); fully distributed in network
notes: Technically sophisticated architecture; genuinely ahead of IBM which had no distributed TP at time. The client-server TP model articulated here proved directionally correct.

metric_value: Simple goal: be number one in distributed production systems; eventually replace mainframes as DEC systems become better at handling large jobs
notes: FAILED: DEC never achieved TP market leadership vs IBM or Tandem; mainframe TP was not replaced by VAX but eventually by open Unix and later Windows server TP platforms. DEC acquired by Compaq 1998.

metric_value: Only company that can integrate TP, office, decision support and timesharing in a single environment; widest range of systems without software changes
notes: Bold differentiation claim vs IBM (multiple OS/TP/DB systems) and Tandem (TP-only narrow focus); partially accurate for 1988 DEC portfolio but overstated competitive moat

metric_value: TP University training program underway; training people to be real experts who can train other experts; Application Centers for Technology demos
notes: Internal capability building for TP market entry; shows DEC's awareness that TP required different sales/support expertise than traditional timesharing business

metric_value: 6600 VAXcluster systems installed worldwide; 16% of all VAX nodes in clusters; 64% of top 200 DEC accounts have VAXcluster installations
notes: Significant installed base demonstrating VAXcluster's maturity and customer acceptance; top 200 accounts figure shows VAXcluster entrenched in largest enterprises

metric_value: High availability VAXcluster reduces downtime to minutes per year vs tens of hours for standalone VAX 8700; achieved via redundant processors and shadowed databases
notes: Technical specification of VAXcluster HA capability; genuinely competitive for 1988; downtime spec of minutes/year was state-of-art (Tandem NonStop was competitive)

metric_value: Distributed TP architecture will cover range from 16 transactions per second (low end) to hundreds of TPS (high end); distribution of stimulus capture + transaction server + DB access
notes: Performance range claim; TPS metrics were key TP competitive metric (TPC-A benchmark introduced 1985); hundreds of TPS was industry-leading for distributed systems in 1988

metric_value: IBM has major mainframe TP market share but multiple OS/TP/DB/network systems, highest cost of ownership, and no distributed TP products today
notes: Accurate competitive assessment for 1988; IBM's CICS/MVS TP environment was indeed expensive and non-distributed; IBM's complexity was genuine weakness DEC sought to exploit

metric_value: HP has four operating systems, emphasizes Unix but no TP applications on Unix today; lacks enterprise-wide solutions, limited networking, lacks clusters or SMP
notes: Accurate for 1988; HP-UX TP ecosystem was thin; HP's subsequent acquisition of Apollo (1989) and Convex strengthened position but DEC's assessment was fair for the moment

metric_value: Tandem is recognized TP leader with magnificent marketing; offers low-cost high-performance expandable TP and good distributed relational DB; but proprietary language/OS and narrow TP focus
notes: Accurate Tandem profile; Tandem NonStop SQL was genuine competitor; DEC's critique of proprietary nature was fair; Tandem acquired by Compaq 1997 then HP, technology lives in HP NonStop

metric_value: EasyNet: one network, 445 locations, 32 countries, 28000 computers, 80000 users; same network for end-user computing, production computing, and software development
notes: DEC's internal enterprise network cited as living proof of concept for their strategy; genuinely impressive for 1988; 'When our customers see what we have, they say that's the direction they want'

metric_value: The mainframe terminal-network model is becoming economically as well as technologically obsolete; mainframe business is not growing; cost of networking rising while distributed computing cost falls
notes: CORRECT PREDICTION: Mainframe growth did stall; distributed computing did displace many mainframe workloads during 1990s. However IBM mainframe survived in high-reliability TP niches — not fully replaced by DEC/VAX but eventually by open client-server and cloud.

metric_value: IBM TP software only works on mainframe — not on PCs or workstations; users doing end-user computing and TP need two sets of devices and two networks
notes: Accurate IBM limitation in 1988; DEC correctly identified this gap; client-server TP was the right direction though DEC lost the war to Unix/RISC and later Windows NT servers

metric_value: In the 1990s most people will need enterprise-wide computing; DEC will be the leader and primary vendor; will be ready with world's best networking, most useful software environment, best desktop products
notes: FAILED: DEC did not become the primary enterprise computing vendor of the 1990s; that role went to HP (Unix/RISC), Sun, and ultimately Microsoft (Windows NT). DEC was acquired by Compaq in 1998 for $9.6B.

metric_value: Peter S. Kastner was DEC Marketing executive who led the work behind the DECtp announcement presented in this document by Bob Glorioso
notes: Context entity; Kastner not a speaker at this meeting but his marketing leadership directly shaped the DECtp strategy and products announced here. Subsequently co-founded Aberdeen Group where he continued to cover TP and enterprise computing.

metric_value: Acquired by Compaq for $9.6 billion in June 1998
notes: DEC never achieved TP market leadership, enterprise computing primacy, or workstation market leadership. Compaq later merged with HP in 2002. VAX/VMS technology survived in niche markets. Notes: actual-outcome row for viability-predictions in this document.
